meta.description: 22 codelists GLOSIS relevantes para petrografia/diagênese/sedimentologia: cementação, weathering, porosidade, estrutura, fragmentos, plasticidade, paisagem.

concepts.definition: Cemented mass cannot be broken in the hands and is continuous (more than 90 percent of soil mass).
Cemented mass cannot be broken by body weight (75-kg standard soil scientist) (more than 90 percent of soil mass).
Cemented mass cannot be broken in the hands but is discontinuous (less than 90 percent of soil mass).
Neither cementation nor compaction observed (slakes in water).
Cemented mass is brittle and hard, but can be broken in the hands.
Compacted mass is appreciably harder or more brittle than other comparable soil mass (slakes in water).
Primary cementing material containing Clay
Primary cementing material containing Clay–sesquioxides
Primary cementing material containing Iron
Primary cementing material containing Iron–manganese (sesquioxides)
Primary cementing material containing Iron–organic matter
Primary cementing material containing Gypsum
Primary cementing material containing Ice
Primary cementing material containing Carbonates
Primary cementing material containing Carbonates–silica
Primary cementing material containing Mechanical
Primary cementing material containing Not known contents
Primary cementing material containing Ploughing
Primary cementing material containing Silica
The layer is largely constructed from cemented nodules or concretions of irregular shape.
The layer is largely constructed from cemented spherical nodules.
The compacted or cemented parts are platelike and have a horizontal or subhorizontal orientation.
The layer has large, equidimensional voids that may be filled with uncemented material.
The layer is less than 50 percent cemented or compacted, and shows a rather irregular appearance.
The layer is more than 90 percent cemented or compacted, and is only interrupted in places by cracks or fissures.
The layer is 50–90 percent cemented or compacted, and in general shows a regular appearance.
Fragments show little or no signs of weathering.
All but the most resistant minerals are weathered, strongly discoloured and altered throughout the fragments, which tend to disintegrate under only moderate pressure.

Partial weathering is indicated by discoloration and loss of crystal form in the outer parts of the fragments while the centres remain relatively fresh and the fragments have lost little of their original strength.
Very low porosity of les than 2%
Low porosity of 2–5%
Medium porosity of 5–15%
High porosity of 15–40%
Very high porosity of more than 40%

Aggregates are observable in place and there is a distinct arrangement of natural surfaces of weakness. When disturbed, the soil material breaks into a mixture of many entire aggregates, some broken aggregates, and little material without aggregates faces. Aggregates surfaces generally show distinct differences with the aggregates interiors.
Moderate to strong

Aggregates are clearly observable in place and there is a prominent arrangement of natural surfaces of weakness. When disturbed, the soil material separates mainly into entire aggregates. Aggregates surfaces generally differ markedly from aggregate interiors.

Aggregates are barely observable in place and there is only a weak arrangement of natural surfaces of weakness. When gently disturbed, the soil material breaks into a mixture of few entire aggregates, many broken aggregates, and much material without aggregate faces. Aggregate surfaces differ in some way from the aggregate interior.
Weak to moderate
Aretfacts in soil measured in (mm)
Coarse artefacts in soil greater than 20mm
Fine artefacts in soild of size between 2mm–6mm
6mm–20mm
Very fine artefacsts in soil of size < 2mm
Combination of boulderds and large boulders
Class combination of rock fragment and artefacts
Combination rock fragments and artefacts of coarse gravel and stones
Combination of fine and medium gravel and artefacts
Combination of medium and course gravels and artefacts
Rock fragments measured in mm
60mm–200mm
Coarse gravel of size  20mm–60mm
Rock fragments of mainly fine gravel of size from 2mm–6mm
Rock fragments mainly in the form of large boulders of size more than 600mm
Combination of rocks and artefacts of medium gravel of size from 6mm–20mm
Rock fragments mainly stones of size from 60mm–200mm
Combination of stone and boulders
Angular rock shape
Flat rock shape
Rounded rock shape
Subrounded rock shape
40%–80% of rock fragments
5%–15% of rock fragments
> 80% of rock fragments
2%–5% of rock fragments
15%–40% of rock fragments
0% of rock fragments
any content, but concentrated at a distinct depth of a horizon
0%–2% of rock fragments
No wire is formable.
Wire formable but breaks if bent into a ring; slight to moderate force required for deformation of the soil mass.
Additioanl plasticity code with combination of  PL and VPL
Wire formable but breaks immediately if bent into a ring; soil mass deformed by very slight force.
Additioanl plasticity code with combination of  SPL and PL
Wire formable and can be bent into a ring; moderately strong to very strong force required for deformation of the soil mass.
After release of pressure, practically no soil material adheres to thumb and finger.
Additional code of combination of SST and ST
After pressure, soil material adheres to both thumb and finger but comes off one or the other rather cleanly. It is not appreciably stretched when the digits are separated.
After pressure, soil material adheres to both thumb and finger and tends to stretch somewhat and pull apart rather than pulling free from either digit.
Additional code of combination of ST and VST
After pressure, soil material adheres strongly to both thumb and finger and is decidedly stretched when they are separated.
Extremely resistant to pressure; cannot be broken in the hands.
Moderately resistant to pressure; can be broken in the hands; not breakable between thumb and forefinger.
Additional code formed by combining HA and VHA
Non-coherent.
Weakly resistant to pressure; easily broken between thumb and forefinger.
slightly hard to hard
Soil mass is very weakly coherent and fragile; breaks to powder or individual grains under very slight pressure.
soft to slightly hard
Very hard Very resistant to pressure; can be broken in the hands only with difficulty.
Soil material crushes only under very strong pressure; cannot be crushed between thumb and forefinger.
Soil material crushes under moderate pressure between thumb and forefinger, but resistance is distinctly noticeable.
Soil material crushes easily under gentle to moderate pressure between thumb and forefinger, and coheres when pressed together.
Non-coherent.
Soil material crushes under strong pressures; barely crushable between thumb and forefinger.
Soil material crushes under very gentle pressure, but coheres when pressed together.

first level of landform is level land (L)
first level of landform is level land (L) and the second level is depression (LD)
first level of landform is level land (L) and the second level is plateau (LL)
first level of landform is level land (L) and the second level is plain (LP)
first level of landform is level land (L) and the second level is valley floor (LV)
first level of landform is sloping land (S)

first level of landform is sloping land (S) and the second level is medium gradient hill (SH)
first level of landform is sloping land (S) and the second level is medium-gradient mountain (SM)
first level of landform is sloping land (S) and the second level is dissected plain (SP)
first level of landform is sloping land (S) and the second level is medium gradient valley (SV)

Position in flat or almost flat terrain: Bottom (drainage line)
Position in undulating to mountainous terrain: Bottom (flat)
Position in undulating to mountainous terrain: Crest (summit)
Position in flat or almost flat terrain: Higher part (rise)
Position in flat or almost flat terrain: Intermediate part (talf)
Position in flat or almost flat terrain: Lower part (and dip)
Position in undulating to mountainous terrain: Lower slope (foot slope)
Position in undulating to mountainous terrain: Middle slope (back slope)
Position in undulating to mountainous terrain: Toe slope
Position in undulating to mountainous terrain: Upper slope (shoulder)
